sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member retention & win-back**: no documented process for churn prevention, re-engagement campaigns, or lapsed-member outreach (high-value gap given gym attrition rates)
- **Complaints & incident handling**: no escalation protocol for member disputes, injury claims, safeguarding concerns, or service failures
- **Financial controls**: no SOP for invoicing discrepancies, refund/cancellation requests, payment plan defaults, or reconciliation workflows
- **Onboarding experience map**: no end-to-end new-member journey SOP (induction, first session, 30-day touchpoints, goal-setting)
- **Health & safety incident reporting**: no template or escalation process for near-misses, equipment damage, or member injuries
- **Staff scheduling & absence cover**: no SOP for shift swaps, emergency cover, or capacity planning during holidays
- **Coach performance review & capability management**: no SOP for feedback cycles, underperformance, or upskilling pathways
- **Supplier & vendor management**: no process for vendor onboarding, contract renewal, or service-level disputes

**Needs updating**

- **Email Newsletter & Blog Post SOPs** (listed as "not built"): structure these as *content calendar* (monthly planning cycle) + *approval workflow* (draft → edit → schedule → send), not ad-hoc
- **Marketing SOPs (2 gaps)**: likely missing paid-ads spend controls, competitor monitoring, or seasonal campaign planning (Q1 New Year, Q3 back-to-routine)
- **Coaches SOP suite**: review for *conflict resolution between coaches*, *member feedback loops*, and *class substitution protocols* — critical in women-only settings where trust & consistency drive retention
- **Client communication SOPs**: add *member communication during

kpi.advice:
# FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE — THE FORGE
## June Trading Review & Forward Actions

---

## 1. INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing drift is real — Average Revenue per Member (AR/M) has collapsed from £170.43 (December) to £120.48 (June).** This isn't member acquisition; it's discounting or class mix erosion. Audit current pricing tiers immediately. Target: return AR/M to £160+ within 2 months via price rises on renewals (+5–8%) and upselling add-ons (personal training packages, nutrition coaching, merchandise).

• **June revenue fell 12.2% month-on-month (£20,198 to £17,710) despite adding 25 members net YTD.** The bigger studio (May-June onwards) should be *growing* revenue, not shrinking it. Immediate actions: (a) launch a "new studio launch" acquisition campaign (referral bonuses, 7-day free trials) to fill the 40%+ empty capacity; (b) introduce class-booking premium tiers (peak-time "VIP' slots at +£3/class).

• **Churn in June was 0% (excellent retention) but May was 5.74%.** Lock in June's momentum: implement a win-back campaign for the 6–7 lapsed members from May, plus monthly retention cadence (birthday discounts, milestone rewards at 3/6/12 months).

---

## 2. REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses are the elephant: May £9,233, June £9,390 — stable but *structurally high* relative to revenue.** Post-relocation, this should be declining as one-off moving costs wash out. Breakdown needed: isolate rent/utilities/insurance/equipment maintenance. If rent on the new studio is >50% of that £9.4k, renegotiate landlord fit-out contribution or explore smaller secondary space for off-peak classes.

• **People costs are proportionate (£3,686 in June = 20.8% of revenue), tracking the industry 18–22% target.** No immediate cut needed, but monitor headcount-to-member ratio: you've added 22 members since May and staffing is flat. Opportunity to absorb growth without extra hires — track utilisation.

• **Quarterly trend (Apr–Jun vs Jan–Mar): non-people expenses averaged £18,447 vs £11,540 in the prior quarter.** Excluding April's relocation capex, May–June non-people costs are still £9,311/month average —

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's Build: Lower (17 classes) to JoJo and Eilis.** JoJo already leads Upper and Full Body; give her the Lower module to complete the Build trilogy. Eilis can co-deliver or shadow to build her technical confidence on a non-Tuesday class, spreading the workload and reducing single-point-of-failure dependency.

• **Move Grace's Elevate (6 classes) and Strongevity (3 classes) entirely to Eilis.** These are lower-intensity, lower-technical modules well-suited to Eilis's current level. This uses her spare capacity and gives her ownership of signature offerings.

• **Shift 15–20 of JoJo's PT sessions to Eilis to rebalance.** JoJo is carrying 44 PTs (67% of her load); Eilis has only 11. Eilis has room and should grow her 1:1 book; this frees JoJo to focus on group delivery and Tuesday technical coaching.

• **Cover Grace's No Sweat Intro (3 sessions) with a rotating rota between JoJo and Eilis** — these are light admin/sales touchpoints, not deep coaching, so sharing them keeps both coaches aware of new member needs.

• **Reassign Grace's 9 Body Composition Scans to whoever has admin time that week** (JoJo or Eilis) — they're not coaching, so use them as low-effort revenue moments when someone has a gap.

• **Review timetable in July to confirm Eilis can sustain 25+ weekly classes and 3–4 PTs per week without burnout**, then lock in the new shape by August.

class_stats.suggestion:
**Fill quiet studio time:**

1. Add a paid "Open Studio" slot on Thursday evenings (your quietest weekday) between PT blocks — members pay £5-8 to use the space unsupervised or with a roaming coach; captures people who want flexibility without a full class commitment.

2. Run a monthly "Games Night" workshop (currently only 14 attendees in 30 days) as a paid skill-building session on a quiet Monday or Thursday — reframe it as "Barbell Technique Clinic" or "Competition Skills Lab" with a small-group cap (8-10 people) at £15-20; gives it purpose beyond a social event.

**Lift the quieter classes:**

3. Move **Engine** and **Capacity** (52 and 62 attendees in 90 days) to Wednesday morning or early evening — your busiest day creates better class energy and peer visibility; pair with a 2-week "Try for Free" invite sent to Build class regulars who might enjoy metabolic conditioning as a complement to strength.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build — Execution Plan for Grace

• **Start with your money first.** Audit last 3 months of transactions — what are members actually *paying for* right now? (Classes, PT rates, packages, add-ons?) This is your real menu. Build the documented version around what's already working, not what you think should exist.

• **Create two separate docs, not one combined.** Members see pricing + benefits (simple, outcome-focused). Coaches get the internal version: margins, upsell sequences, what bundles you're pushing this quarter, commission structure if applicable. They're completely different sales conversations.

• **Price by clarity, not guessing.** For each offering, write: "A member gets X sessions/month + Y benefit + Z turnaround time = £price." If you can't complete that sentence cleanly, the service isn't ready to sell. This forces you to stop offering vague things.

• **Assign one person (you?) to own pricing changes.** Coaches shouldn't negotiate rates with individual members. Create a simple approval process: coach suggests discount → they send you the reason → you approve/deny. Protects margins and prevents undercutting.

• **Test it with 5 members this week.** Share the member menu with your top 5 most-engaged members. Ask: "Does this match what you thought you were paying for?" Adjust based on feedback before rolling out widely.

Done right, this pays for itself immediately through clearer upsells and fewer billing disputes.

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**1. Mine your actual customer conversations NOW**
- Pull the last 50 enquiries/calls/DMs. What objections come up? ("Is it really women-only?" "Do I need experience?" "What if I'm not fit?"). Build FAQ directly from these — not guesses.
- Assign this to whoever handles member queries today. 90 minutes of work, massive payoff.

**2. Lead with the unique positioning in the hero section**
- Your first sentence should answer: "Why women-only?" Don't bury it. Something like "Training without the gym-bro atmosphere — designed for women, by women" hits different than generic fitness copy.
- Test this against your current page. If a visitor can't immediately say what makes The Forge different, rewrite it.

**3. Create a "real clarity" section addressing real hesitation points**
- Use sub-headings like: "New to fitness?" / "Do I need equipment?" / "What's the vibe actually like?" with 2-3 sentence answers.
- Use member testimonials as proof here (not praise — use quotes about the *specific experience*: "I felt safe asking beginner questions" beats "Great gym!").

**4. Add conversion blockers section at bottom**
- Before the CTA, address: cost (transparent pricing), commitment (can I try a session?), schedule (hours that work for Belfast women).
- Link directly to booking or free trial — don't make people hunt for it.

**What to measure:** Track which FAQ items get clicked most + test two headline versions. Change informs your actual sales messaging next.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Member Feedback System — Execution Plan

**Immediate (This Week):**
- **Use Google Forms or Typeform** — takes 20 minutes, free, auto-organizes responses. Post the link in your WhatsApp group, email blast, and as a QR code by the exit. No custom platform needed.
- **Ask 3 specific questions only** — "What's working? What's frustrating? What would bring a friend?" Generic satisfaction scores are useless; these drive decisions.

**Embed It Into Routine (Not Extra):**
- **Piggyback on post-class chaos** — hand out printed feedback cards during the last 2 minutes of your busiest class (likely Thursday/Friday evening). Pen + card takes 60 seconds. More responses than an email ever will.
- **Assign one member as "feedback champion"** per shift — they collect cards, drop them in a physical box. Gives her ownership, keeps it friction-free for you.

**Close the Loop (Critical):**
- **Share one insight + one action taken monthly** via your WhatsApp/Instagram story or email. Example: "You said changing times was hard → We're adding a 6am slot in March." Members who see feedback → action = they keep giving feedback AND feel heard (retention goldmine).

**Why this works for The Forge specifically:** Women's fitness is loyalty-driven. A real feedback system (even basic) positions you as genuinely listening when competitors aren't. One actionable insight beats a 50-question survey you'll ignore.

What's your highest-traffic class time? Start there.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Plan for The Forge

**1. Audit existing workflows FIRST (before writing anything)**
- Spend 2 hours shadowing staff delivering each service. Document what they *actually do* vs. what they think they do. This gap is where most SOPs fail. You'll write better docs because you've seen the chaos points.

**2. Create a single SOP template with these 5 sections ONLY**
- Customer journey (touchpoint by touchpoint)
- Staff responsibilities at each stage
- Decision trees (what happens if X occurs?)
- Tools/resources needed (links, templates, passwords)
- Success metrics (how do you know it worked?)
- Use Google Docs with comments enabled so staff can flag what's unrealistic *before* you finalize.

**3. Sequence by dependency, not importance**
- Start with **Membership** (it feeds everyone else), then **Personal Training** (revenue driver), then Fuel Forward/Well75/Strongevity. Why? Because members go through membership/PT first—those SOPs will be stress-tested immediately, revealing what template adjustments you need.

**4. Build accountability into the SOP itself**
- Assign ONE person as "owner" for each SOP (they maintain it quarterly). Make them sign off. SOPs that aren't owned become outdated in 6 weeks.

**5. Test ruthlessly with frontline staff**
- Have a new team member execute the SOP cold with zero explanation. Where they get stuck = your documentation gap. Fix *that*, not the SOP process itself.

**Realistic timeline: 4 weeks start to finish.**

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Use a free template TODAY** — grab Notion or Canva's free calendar template, not a paid tool. Map 12 weeks out in a single view. This takes 90 minutes and removes the "what to post" paralysis that kills consistency.

• **Batch-create content in 2-hour sprints** — film 8-12 short videos of members mid-workout, transformations, or quick form tips in ONE session monthly. Schedule them across weeks. This destroys the daily posting friction that derails most gym owners.

• **Anchor posts to revenue drivers, not vanity** — 60% of posts should drive trial memberships or class bookings (class highlights, "try this free," member wins). 40% engagement/community. Track which formats get actual inquiries via unique landing pages or promo codes in captions.

• **Assign ONE person (likely you initially) as the weekly reviewer** — every Sunday, spend 20 minutes confirming next week's 3-4 posts are scheduled in Buffer/Meta Business Suite. Not negotiable; this catches gaps before they happen.

• **Repurpose ruthlessly** — one workout tip becomes: Instagram Reel + carousel post + TikTok + Stories. One member testimonial becomes: quote graphic + video clip + featured story. This cuts actual content creation load in half.

**Start this week: pick your platform (likely Instagram for women's fitness), template it, and schedule Week 1's posts by Friday.**

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing The Videography Schedule with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW as a standing appointment** — Don't email back-and-forth. Call Jordan today, agree on a specific day (e.g., "first Tuesday of every 8-week cycle"), block it on both calendars, and set a recurring reminder 2 weeks before. Treat it like a client with a contract, not a vague intention.

• **Have a content shot-list ready before each session** — Don't waste Jordan's time figuring out what to film. 48 hours before, send 5-7 specific clips you need: member transformation story, new class format, behind-the-scenes content, testimonial interview. This keeps costs tight and output usable immediately.

• **Assign ONE person to manage day-of logistics** — Member availability, gym setup, lighting checks, audio. You running the gym AND directing Jordan = neither gets your focus. Make this someone's 2-hour responsibility each cycle.

• **Repurpose ruthlessly across 8 weeks** — One day of filming should produce 12-15 short clips for Instagram, TikTok, email, website, and ads. Build a content calendar the moment you get raw footage so nothing sits unused.

• **Pre-agree on deliverables and payment terms** — Fixed price per session? Hourly? Revision rounds included? Get it written down now so there's no negotiation friction that delays shoots or kills momentum.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Concrete Action Plan for 6-Week Trial Automations

• **Audit what's breaking now** — Pull your last 20 trial enquiries. Track where drop-off happens (email unanswered? payment friction? unclear next steps?). Fix *those specific gaps* first before automating broken processes.

• **Build a 3-email sequence, not more** — Day 1: Welcome + trial booking link (make it 2-clicks max, no form friction). Day 3: "Here's what to bring" + gym tour video (builds confidence, reduces no-shows). Day 5: "See you tomorrow" + parking/entry details. Automate in your booking platform (Mindbody, Zen, whatever you use).

• **Lock in the payment friction point** — Decide: Do they pay upfront (higher conversion killer) or at first session? If upfront, embed a Stripe/PayPal button in that welcome email with a reason ("secures your spot"). If at session, add a manual reminder 48hrs before so you're not chasing payment on the day.

• **Set ONE metric to track** — Not "enquiries converted" (too vague). Track: % who book a trial slot within 24hrs of enquiry. This tells you if your automation sequence is compelling. Target: 70%+.

• **Assign someone to monitor 2 weeks in** — Automations aren't "set and forget." After 2 weeks, check: Are emails actually sending? Any bounces? Is booking link working? One person, 15 mins/week.

**Action today:** Audit those 20 enquiries. What's the actual bottleneck?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Specific Action Checklists for The Forge

**1. Start with output, work backwards to tasks**
Don't list duties—define what "winning" looks like for each role first. Example: JoJo owns "member retention." Her KPI is "95% monthly renewal rate." Now reverse-engineer: what 3-4 weekly actions guarantee that? (Exit interviews with cancellations, retention call list, referral tracking). This prevents busy-work checklists.

**2. Make KPIs measurable by Friday EOD**
Each person needs numbers they can actually hit weekly, not vague targets. "Eilis: conduct 4 sales consultations" beats "improve conversions." Sarah might own "deliver 3 new referral partnerships contacted" or "process payroll + health/safety check." Trackable in 60 seconds.

**3. Use a shared visible tracker, not a document**
Create a simple Google Sheet or Asana board where each role owns their row. They log completion + the number each week. Grace sees it instantly. This creates peer accountability without micromanagement—and shows patterns ("JoJo always hits Monday, Sarah struggles Wednesday mornings").

**4. Set one "flex task" per role for problem-solving**
Don't lock them to rigid checklists. Give JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah each one slot per week to tackle emerging friction (member complaint patterns, equipment gaps, staff scheduling issues). Turns checklist into a growth tool.

**5. Review & refine in 4-week sprints**
Lock the checklist in place for a month, then spend 30 mins together looking at what moved the needle. Kill tasks that don

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Run Your Team Vision Session — The Execution Play

• **Pre-session: Send the SSO + 90-day plan 48 hours early** — not to soften them up, but so they're not hearing it for the first time live. They'll ask smarter questions, you'll save 20 minutes of confusion, and people feel respected when they can actually prepare. Include one sentence per person showing how *their specific role* connects to the 90-day wins.

• **Open with the brutal truth first** — spend 5 mins on where The Forge is now vs. where it needs to be by Day 90. Numbers. Gap. This isn't motivational; it's context-setting so "your lane" doesn't feel arbitrary. "We're £X short of cash flow targets, so member retention and class fill rates are non-negotiable."

• **Assign lanes by impact, not job title** — don't just say "you own retention." Be specific: "Sarah, you own retention via member check-ins + referral system — here's the 90-day target: 5 new referrals/week, 0 cancellations in your cohort." Each person needs one clear, measurable outcome they can't wiggle out of.

• **End with the weekly checkpoint rhythm** — "We're reviewing progress every Friday, 15 mins, same time." No ambiguity on how you'll track it. People commit harder when they know you're actually watching.

• **Record it (audio or video) for anyone who can't attend** — one person missing kills momentum. Make it asynchronous-friendly.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge

• **Map actual hours, not job titles (this week).** Have each team member track where time *actually* goes for 5 days — classes taught, admin, cleaning, member check-ins, sales calls. You'll spot the person doing sales + deep-cleaning + scheduling simultaneously. Generic job descriptions hide overload.

• **Identify the single bottleneck person.** In small gyms, one person usually blocks everything (likely your operations/admin person). Ask: "If [person] got sick tomorrow, what stops?" That's your over-stretched anchor. Start there.

• **Pillar swap one high-impact task immediately.** Don't wait 30 days to rebalance everything. Pick ONE task the over-stretched person hates or that's eating disproportionate time (e.g., member admin, class scheduling) and move it to someone with capacity this week. Instant relief signals you're serious.

• **Build a simple "coverage grid"** for the next 30 days showing who owns what on which days. Make it visible. Rotating one person off "always available" status and giving them set hours shifts psychology and prevents mission creep.

• **Measure the win:** By day 30, ask over-stretched team member what *new* thing they could do or improve if they had 5 spare hours/week. Let them own it. That's your retention play—they feel capacity, not just relief.

What's your gut sense of who's drowning right now?

# Cost-Cutting Execution for The Forge

• **Audit the big three immediately** — Staff scheduling (shift overlap waste), utility spend (gym peak hours vs. off-peak contracts), and supplier contracts (cleaning, equipment maintenance). These typically account for 60-70% of gym operating costs. Get quotes from 2-3 competitors' suppliers TODAY to benchmark.

• **Renegotiate your largest recurring vendor contracts NOW** — Equipment maintenance, cleaning, internet/utilities. Most give 10-15% discounts for multi-year commitments or bundling services. Tell suppliers you're evaluating alternatives (you are). Aim to land 2-3 quick wins here.

• **Cut unprofitable class slots, not staff** — Don't reduce headcount blindly. Identify which class times have <60% capacity and consolidate them. Redeploy those instructors to high-demand slots or 1-on-1 PT (higher margin). Track revenue-per-labor-hour, not just attendance.

• **Implement a 90-day energy efficiency sprint** — LED lighting upgrade, programmable thermostats, motion sensors. This nets 15-20% utility savings for gyms and often qualifies for local council grants in Northern Ireland. Costs £2-3k upfront but pays back in 6-8 months.

• **Switch to variable cost model where possible** — Move from fixed cleaning contracts to performance-based (pay per visit vs. standing fee). Same for laundry—outsource rather than in-house if volume doesn't justify it. Protects margin during slower months.

**Target the 10% with a data-first approach—measure before cutting.**

# Updating Monthly Finance KPIs at The Forge

• **Lock a specific day (e.g., first Monday post-month-end) and do it in 90 minutes max** — Pull last month's bank statement, membership software, and PT bookings data simultaneously. Don't spread this over a week; batching kills momentum and creates errors.

• **Track these 5 metrics only** (ruthlessly ignore the rest): Monthly Recurring Revenue (memberships + PT packages), cash burn rate, membership churn %, acquisition cost per new member, and gross margin on services. Anything beyond this dilutes focus for a gym operator.

• **Build a simple one-page Google Sheet comparing Month-to-Date vs. Last Month vs. Target** — use conditional formatting (red/green cells). One glance tells Grace if she's trending up or needs to pivot marketing spend that week. Update it live as payments hit the account.

• **Automate the boring bits now** — Connect your membership software to a tool like Zapier so member/revenue data feeds automatically into your sheet. Saves you 20 minutes monthly and kills data-entry errors that kill credibility with your bank.

• **Flag ONE number for immediate action** — If churn spiked, retention effort starts this week. If acquisition cost jumped, pause that ad campaign. Don't just track; react within 5 days of noticing the shift.

What's your current biggest KPI pain point — is it visibility, accuracy, or acting on the data?

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Plan

**1. Pick your baseline metric TODAY — revenue per member per month**
Don't build a spreadsheet first. Calculate: last month's total revenue ÷ current member count. This single number is your anchor. Everything forecasts from here. Once you have it, you've started.

**2. Map your three revenue levers only**
Most gyms obsess over too many variables. For The Forge, focus exclusively on: (a) membership count trajectory, (b) class pack sales (non-members), (c) PT/personal training uptake. Forecast each separately for 12 months. Ignore ancillary income until you've nailed these three.

**3. Build a "growth scenario" not a "best guess"**
Create three columns: Conservative (lose 5% members, flat PT income), Base (current trends continue), Aggressive (implement one specific action — e.g., referral incentive — and model the lift). This prevents overconfidence and gives you decision points when reality diverges.

**4. Set a monthly review checkpoint — same day every month**
Actual vs. forecast on those three levers. 15 minutes max. When real numbers drift from forecast, that's your signal to adjust strategy, not panic. This is the only way forecasting becomes useful (not a document that gathers dust).

**5. Link to one operational decision you'll make in Q1**
If forecasting shows PT income stalling, you're hiring a PT coach or changing commission structure. Decide now what insight would trigger what action. That's how this drives growth.

**When?** Start with the spreadsheet this week. Review first numbers mid-January.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge — Execution Plan

**1. Get your numbers in 48 hours, not perfect later**
Pull last 3 months of data from your booking system/till: revenue per class type, PT sessions, and add-ons (shakes, merchandise, guest passes). Don't wait for cleaned data—use what's live. You need directional truth now, not audit-perfect numbers.

**2. Calculate real contribution margin per service (revenue minus direct costs only)**
- **Classes**: Revenue ÷ attendee count = per-person revenue. Subtract instructor wages + music/platform costs. Ignore rent (it's fixed).
- **PT**: Session rate minus trainer commission/wages.
- **Add-ons**: Near-100% margin—just flag your supplier costs.
This shows you which service actually makes money *per transaction*.

**3. Layer in capacity utilization (this changes everything)**
A class generating £200 revenue at 8/20 capacity is weaker than you think. Calculate: *Revenue per available slot*. You'll likely find some off-peak classes are dragging profit down despite decent per-person rates.

**4. Identify your "profit lever" immediately**
Whichever service has highest contribution margin + consistent demand = focus zone for next 60 days. (Bet: it's PT, not classes. If PT margins are weak, that's your problem to solve first.)

**5. Build your one-page P&L now**
Rows: Classes (by type), PT, Add-ons. Columns: Revenue, Direct Costs, Contribution Margin, % Margin, Capacity %. Share it with your team—

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan for Automation Audit

• **Map the full funnel first (30 mins max):** Screenshot every automation touchpoint from enquiry landing to first class booking. You'll spot duplicates instantly—likely welcome email sequences firing twice, or nurture campaigns overlapping. Use this visual map as your audit checklist.

• **Test as a fake enquiry TODAY:** Submit a form yourself using a test email address. Actually go through the entire flow. This catches broken links, missing steps, or emails that never arrive—theory won't show you this. Note every delay and gap.

• **Identify the ONE critical choke point:** In most gyms it's the "confirm trial booking" step. Does someone manually text her, or does an automated SMS go out? Does she get a reminder 24hrs before? Fix this first—it directly impacts show-up rates. Everything else is secondary.

• **Delete ruthlessly, not hesitantly:** If two automations do similar jobs, nuke one completely. Confused messaging kills conversions. It's not "nice to have both"—it's noise. Document what you're removing so you can resurrect if needed.

• **Set a weekly 15-min health check:** One person (probably you) reviews the funnel every Monday. Watch for automations that drift or fail silently. Audits are pointless if you're back here in 6 months.

**Priority outcome:** Know your real conversion rate from enquiry → trial completion. You probably don't right now.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Execution

**1. ID your 3 highest-ROI tasks (do this today)**
- **Membership follow-ups**: Track which members go dark after sign-up or month 3 — automate "we miss you" SMS/email sequences triggered by inactivity. This directly drives retention revenue.
- **Class booking reminders + no-show reduction**: Auto-send 24h reminders + day-of SMS. Reduces no-shows by 15-25%, improving class viability data so you know what sticks.
- **Lead nurture for trial bookings**: Auto-qualify inquiries (budget, goals, availability), send tailored class recommendations, track responses. Converts tire-kickers faster without your team manually qualifying.

**2. Use a plug-and-play tool — don't build from scratch**
- **Zapier** (easiest entry point): Connect your booking system (likely Mindbody or similar) + email/SMS + a CRM. Pre-built templates for fitness exist. Cost: ~£20-50/month.
- **Alternative if you want SMS muscle**: **Twilio** (more control, slightly steeper learning curve) or **HubSpot free tier** for email sequences. HubSpot integrates SMS too.

**3. Set the trigger → action in 90 minutes max**
- Write out the exact trigger ("member hasn't booked in 14 days") → exact message ("Hey [name], try our new mobility class Tuesday") → delivery channel + timing. Don't overthink copy.
- Test with 50 people first. Measure: open rate, click rate, re-bookings.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cyber Security for The Forge Website – Action Plan

• **Audit first, then act** – Run a free security scan (use Sucuri or SSL Labs) before spending money. This tells you exactly what's broken (unpatched plugins, missing SSL, weak passwords). Grace likely has low-hanging fruit costing £0 to fix.

• **Get SSL sorted THIS WEEK** – If the site isn't HTTPS, fix it now. It's £30-60/year, takes 30 mins with your hosting provider, and directly affects Google rankings + customer trust (especially when collecting payment/member data).

• **Lock down the basics immediately** – Change ALL default admin passwords, remove unused plugins/themes (they're breach vectors), and enable two-factor authentication on the WordPress/CMS login. Most breaches exploit neglected admin access, not sophisticated hacks.

• **Automate backups + get a security plugin** – Use UpdraftPlus (free version = daily backups to cloud). Then layer in Wordfence (free tier) for real-time threat monitoring. Cost: £0-10/month. This prevents total catastrophe if something does get compromised.

• **Plan one quarterly security review** – Block 30 mins every 3 months to check plugin updates, review user access, and audit login attempts. This beats panic-mode crisis management and takes less time than dealing with a breach.

**Bottom line:** Spend this week on the £0 fixes. Then allocate £15-25/month for ongoing protection. Don't overcomplicate it.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

- Your cost per lead has ballooned 4.5x in three months (£6 to £27), whilst absolute lead volume has collapsed 94% (69 to 4). This is a catastrophic decline, not a gradual efficiency trade-off. April's £6/lead was genuinely strong; you're not optimising—you're broken.
- Close rate has *improved* dramatically (29% in April → 75% in July), suggesting creative quality or audience relevance isn't the problem. The issue is reach/frequency collapse: you're either massively underspending, throttled by Meta's learning phase, or running against exhausted audiences with no creative rotation.
- July's £109 spend is a red flag. You've either paused campaigns, let budgets run dry, or pulled back after seeing June's £21/lead and panicked. That micro-budget guarantees poor algorithm performance on Meta—you need minimum £15–20/day per active campaign to sustain learning.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately restore spend to £400–500 for August** (roughly April–May levels). Run it evenly across the month (£13–17/day minimum). Your close rate proves the offer converts; you're starving the algorithm. Track daily performance to catch any regressions early.

2. **Audit and refresh creative ruthlessly.** Pull performance data from April (when CPL was £6) and identify which 3–5 creatives drove that. Rebuild variants: new hooks, different angles on the offer (speed of results, community, transformation), testimonial/before-afters from recent customers. Pause anything over 2 months old.

3. **Test audience segmentation.** Split budget 60/40: 60% to cold lookalikes of your best converters (sales in Apr/May), 40% to a narrower interest stack (e.g. "women, 25–45, fitness + wellness + self-improvement, Belfast + 5 miles"). Your 75% close rate suggests your audience is warm; you may just be reaching the wrong people now due to ad fatigue or algorithmic drift.

4. **Single biggest lever: crank the budget back up to April levels and commit for 3
